They have become my enemies.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Search me, God, and know my heart.
Try me, and know my thoughts.
verses.explainer: Search me, God, and know my heart; test me and know my anxious thoughts. Having described God's comprehensive knowledge (vv.1-18), David now INVITES that searching. He wants to be known, not hidden.

verses.text:
See if there is any wicked way in me,
and lead me in the everlasting way.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

explainer.summary: A psalm of lament crying out to God in distress, seeking deliverance, and affirming trust despite difficult circumstances.
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 140 belongs to the lament genre -- the largest category in the Psalter, reflecting the reality that much of prayer is born from pain.

verses.text:
Deliver me, Yahweh, from the evil man.
Preserve me from the violent man;
verses.explainer: A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

verses.text:
those who devise mischief in their hearts.
They continually gather themselves together for war.
verses.explainer: A verse addressing the heart -- the center of thought, will, and emotion in Hebrew understanding. The Psalms consistently teach that authentic worship begins in the inner life, not in external ritual.

verses.text:
They have sharpened their tongues like a serpent.
Viper’s poison is under their lips.
Selah.
verses.explainer: This verse includes 'Selah' -- a musical or liturgical instruction whose exact meaning is uncertain. It may indicate a pause for reflection, a musical interlude, or a signal to lift the voice or instruments.

verses.text:
Yahweh, keep me from the hands of the wicked.
Preserve me from the violent men who have determined to trip my feet.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
The proud have hidden a snare for me,
they have spread the cords of a net by the path.
They have set traps for me.
Selah.
verses.explainer: This verse includes 'Selah' -- a musical or liturgical instruction whose exact meaning is uncertain. It may indicate a pause for reflection, a musical interlude, or a signal to lift the voice or instruments.

verses.text:
I said to Yahweh, “You are my God.”
Listen to the cry of my petitions, Yahweh.
verses.explainer: A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

verses.text:
Yahweh, the Lord, the strength of my salvation,
you have covered my head in the day of battle.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Yahweh, don’t grant the desires of the wicked.
Don’t let their evil plans succeed, or they will become proud.
Selah.
verses.explainer: This verse includes 'Selah' -- a musical or liturgical instruction whose exact meaning is uncertain. It may indicate a pause for reflection, a musical interlude, or a signal to lift the voice or instruments.

verses.text:
As for the head of those who surround me,
let the mischief of their own lips cover them.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Let burning coals fall on them.
Let them be thrown into the fire,
into miry pits, from where they never rise.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
An evil speaker won’t be established in the earth.
Evil will hunt the violent man to overthrow him.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
I know that Yahweh will maintain the cause of the afflicted,
and justice for the needy.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Surely the righteous will give thanks to your name.
The upright will dwell in your presence.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

explainer.summary: A psalm of lament crying out to God in distress, seeking deliverance, and affirming trust despite difficult circumstances.
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 141 belongs to the lament genre -- the largest category in the Psalter, reflecting the reality that much of prayer is born from pain.

verses.text:
Yahweh, I have called on you.
Come to me quickly!
Listen to my voice when I call to you.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Let my prayer be set before you like incense;
the lifting up of my hands like the evening sacrifice.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Set a watch, Yahweh, before my mouth.
Keep the door of my lips.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Don’t incline my heart to any evil thing,
to practice deeds of wickedness with men who work iniquity.
Don’t let me eat of their delicacies.
verses.explainer: A verse addressing the heart -- the center of thought, will, and emotion in Hebrew understanding. The Psalms consistently teach that authentic worship begins in the inner life, not in external ritual.

A contrast between the righteous and the wicked that reflects the Psalter's moral framework: choices matter, character has consequences, and God sees the difference between those who seek Him and those who reject Him.

verses.text:
Their judges are thrown down by the sides of the rock.
They will hear my words, for they are well spoken.
verses.explainer: Rock and mountain imagery representing stability, permanence, and strength. God is the 'Rock' -- the unshakable foundation when everything else is uncertain.

verses.text:
“As when one plows and breaks up the earth,
our bones are scattered at the mouth of Sheol.”
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
For my eyes are on you, Yahweh, the Lord.
In you, I take refuge.
Don’t leave my soul destitute.
verses.explainer: An expression of trust in God as refuge and protection. The Psalms repeatedly use fortress imagery -- God is a stronghold, a rock, a shield -- because the ancient world understood safety in terms of physical fortification.

verses.text:
Keep me from the snare which they have laid for me,
from the traps of the workers of iniquity.
verses.explainer: A verse dealing with human sinfulness. The Psalms handle sin with both searing honesty (acknowledging its reality) and confident hope (appealing to God's forgiveness).

verses.text:
Let the wicked fall together into their own nets,
while I pass by.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

explainer.summary: A psalm of lament crying out to God in distress, seeking deliverance, and affirming trust despite difficult circumstances.
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 142 belongs to the lament genre -- the largest category in the Psalter, reflecting the reality that much of prayer is born from pain.

verses.text:
I cry with my voice to Yahweh.
With my voice, I ask Yahweh for mercy.
verses.explainer: An appeal to or celebration of God's steadfast love (chesed) -- His covenant faithfulness that endures regardless of human failure. Chesed is the most important word in the Psalms for describing God's character.

verses.text:
I pour out my complaint before him.
I tell him my troubles.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
When my spirit was overwhelmed within me,
you knew my route.
On the path in which I walk,
they have hidden a snare for me.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Look on my right, and see;
for there is no one who is concerned for me.
Refuge has fled from me.
No one cares for my soul.
verses.explainer: An expression of trust in God as refuge and protection. The Psalms repeatedly use fortress imagery -- God is a stronghold, a rock, a shield -- because the ancient world understood safety in terms of physical fortification.

verses.text:
I cried to you, Yahweh.
I said, “You are my refuge,
my portion in the land of the living.”
verses.explainer: An expression of trust in God as refuge and protection. The Psalms repeatedly use fortress imagery -- God is a stronghold, a rock, a shield -- because the ancient world understood safety in terms of physical fortification.

verses.text:
Listen to my cry,
for I am in desperate need.
deliver me from my persecutors,
For they are stronger than me.
verses.explainer: A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

verses.text:
Bring my soul out of prison,
that I may give thanks to your name.
The righteous will surround me,
for you will be good to me.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

explainer.summary: A psalm of lament crying out to God in distress, seeking deliverance, and affirming trust despite difficult circumstances.
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 143 belongs to the lament genre -- the largest category in the Psalter, reflecting the reality that much of prayer is born from pain.

verses.text:
Hear my prayer, Yahweh.
Listen to my petitions.
In your faithfulness and righteousness, relieve me.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Don’t enter into judgment with your servant,
for in your sight no man living is righteous.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
For the enemy pursues my soul.
He has struck my life down to the ground.
He has made me live in dark places, as those who have been long dead.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Therefore my spirit is overwhelmed within me.
My heart within me is desolate.
verses.explainer: A verse addressing the heart -- the center of thought, will, and emotion in Hebrew understanding. The Psalms consistently teach that authentic worship begins in the inner life, not in external ritual.

verses.text:
I remember the days of old.
I meditate on all your doings.
I contemplate the work of your hands.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
I spread out my hands to you.
My soul thirsts for you, like a parched land.
Selah.
verses.explainer: This verse includes 'Selah' -- a musical or liturgical instruction whose exact meaning is uncertain. It may indicate a pause for reflection, a musical interlude, or a signal to lift the voice or instruments.

verses.text:
Hurry to answer me, Yahweh.
My spirit fails.
Don’t hide your face from me,
so that I don’t become like those who go down into the pit.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Cause me to hear your loving kindness in the morning,
for I trust in you.
Cause me to know the way in which I should walk,
for I lift up my soul to you.
verses.explainer: An appeal to or celebration of God's steadfast love (chesed) -- His covenant faithfulness that endures regardless of human failure. Chesed is the most important word in the Psalms for describing God's character.

verses.text:
Deliver me, Yahweh, from my enemies.
I flee to you to hide me.
verses.explainer: A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

verses.text:
Teach me to do your will,
for you are my God.
Your Spirit is good.
Lead me in the land of uprightness.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
Revive me, Yahweh, for your name’s sake.
In your righteousness, bring my soul out of trouble.
verses.explainer: The psalmist pours out honest complaint before God. Biblical lament is not lack of faith but an expression of it -- bringing pain to God because you believe He cares and can act.

verses.text:
In your loving kindness, cut off my enemies,
and destroy all those who afflict my soul,
For I am your servant.
verses.explainer: An appeal to or celebration of God's steadfast love (chesed) -- His covenant faithfulness that endures regardless of human failure. Chesed is the most important word in the Psalms for describing God's character.

explainer.summary: A royal psalm concerning the Davidic king, God's anointed ruler, and the promises attached to the throne of David.
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 144 is a royal psalm originally celebrating the Davidic king but understood by Christians as pointing to Christ, the ultimate King.

verses.text:
Blessed be Yahweh, my rock,
who teaches my hands to war,
and my fingers to battle:
verses.explainer: Rock and mountain imagery representing stability, permanence, and strength. God is the 'Rock' -- the unshakable foundation when everything else is uncertain.

verses.text:
my loving kindness, my fortress,
my high tower, my deliverer,
my shield, and he in whom I take refuge;
who subdues my people under me.
verses.explainer: An appeal to or celebration of God's steadfast love (chesed) -- His covenant faithfulness that endures regardless of human failure. Chesed is the most important word in the Psalms for describing God's character.

verses.text:
Yahweh, what is man, that you care for him?
Or the son of man, that you think of him?
verses.explainer: A verse concerning the king -- God's anointed representative who embodies divine justice on earth. Christians read royal psalms as ultimately pointing to Christ.

verses.text:
Man is like a breath.
His days are like a shadow that passes away.
verses.explainer: A verse concerning the king -- God's anointed representative who embodies divine justice on earth. Christians read royal psalms as ultimately pointing to Christ.

verses.text:
Part your heavens, Yahweh, and come down.
Touch the mountains, and they will smoke.
verses.explainer: A reference to God's heavenly throne and sovereign rule. The Psalms affirm that God governs from heaven while actively engaging with events on earth -- He is both transcendent and intimately involved.

verses.text:
Throw out lightning, and scatter them.
Send out your arrows, and rout them.
verses.explainer: A verse concerning the king -- God's anointed representative who embodies divine justice on earth. Christians read royal psalms as ultimately pointing to Christ.
A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

verses.text:
whose mouths speak deceit,
Whose right hand is a right hand of falsehood.
verses.explainer: A verse concerning the king -- God's anointed representative who embodies divine justice on earth. Christians read royal psalms as ultimately pointing to Christ.

verses.text:
I will sing a new song to you, God.
On a ten-stringed lyre, I will sing praises to you.
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
You are he who gives salvation to kings,
who rescues David, his servant, from the deadly sword.
verses.explainer: A reference to kingship -- either God's universal rule or the human Davidic king who represents God's authority on earth. Royal psalms celebrate the ideal of just, God-honoring governance.

verses.text:
Rescue me, and deliver me out of the hands of foreigners,
whose mouths speak deceit,
whose right hand is a right hand of falsehood.
verses.explainer: A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

verses.text:
Then our sons will be like well-nurtured plants,
our daughters like pillars carved to adorn a palace.
verses.explainer: A verse concerning the king -- God's anointed representative who embodies divine justice on earth. Christians read royal psalms as ultimately pointing to Christ.

verses.text:
Our barns are full, filled with all kinds of provision.
Our sheep produce thousands and ten thousands in our fields.
verses.explainer: A verse concerning the king -- God's anointed representative who embodies divine justice on earth. Christians read royal psalms as ultimately pointing to Christ.

verses.text:
Our oxen will pull heavy loads.
There is no breaking in, and no going away,
and no outcry in our streets.
verses.explainer: A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

verses.text:
Happy are the people who are in such a situation.
Happy are the people whose God is Yahweh.
verses.explainer: A verse concerning the king -- God's anointed representative who embodies divine justice on earth. Christians read royal psalms as ultimately pointing to Christ.

explainer.summary: I will exalt you, my God the King. Every day I will praise you. Great is the LORD and most worthy of praise. The LORD is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and rich in love. The LORD is faithful to all His promises. He is near to all who call on Him.
themes: God near to all who call
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 145 is the last of David's psalms and a comprehensive portrait of God's character: great, gracious, compassionate, faithful, and near. It sets up the final 'Hallelujah' chorus of Psalms 146-150.

verses.text:
I will exalt you, my God, the King.
I will praise your name forever and ever.
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
Every day I will praise you.
I will extol your name forever and ever.
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
Great is Yahweh, and greatly to be praised!
His greatness is unsearchable.
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
One generation will commend your works to another,
and will declare your mighty acts.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Of the glorious majesty of your honor,
of your wondrous works, I will meditate.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Men will speak of the might of your awesome acts.
I will declare your greatness.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
They will utter the memory of your great goodness,
and will sing of your righteousness.
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
Yahweh is gracious, merciful,
slow to anger, and of great loving kindness.
verses.explainer: An appeal to or celebration of God's steadfast love (chesed) -- His covenant faithfulness that endures regardless of human failure. Chesed is the most important word in the Psalms for describing God's character.

verses.text:
Yahweh is good to all.
His tender mercies are over all his works.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
All your works will give thanks to you, Yahweh.
Your saints will extol you.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
They will speak of the glory of your kingdom,
and talk about your power;
verses.explainer: A reference to kingship -- either God's universal rule or the human Davidic king who represents God's authority on earth. Royal psalms celebrate the ideal of just, God-honoring governance.

verses.text:
to make known to the sons of men his mighty acts,
the glory of the majesty of his kingdom.
verses.explainer: A reference to kingship -- either God's universal rule or the human Davidic king who represents God's authority on earth. Royal psalms celebrate the ideal of just, God-honoring governance.

verses.text:
Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom.
Your dominion endures throughout all generations.
Yahweh is faithful in all his words,
and loving in all his deeds.
verses.explainer: An appeal to or celebration of God's steadfast love (chesed) -- His covenant faithfulness that endures regardless of human failure. Chesed is the most important word in the Psalms for describing God's character.

verses.text:
Yahweh upholds all who fall,
and raises up all those who are bowed down.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
The eyes of all wait for you.
You give them their food in due season.
verses.explainer: Water imagery in the Psalms carries dual meaning: life-giving provision (streams, rain, still waters) and chaotic threat (floods, depths, raging seas). The psalmist trusts God over both.

verses.text:
You open your hand,
and satisfy the desire of every living thing.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Yahweh is righteous in all his ways,
and gracious in all his works.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Yahweh is near to all those who call on him,
to all who call on him in truth.
verses.explainer: The LORD is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in truth. God's nearness is available to everyone, but it requires genuine, truthful calling -- not casual or manipulative prayer.

verses.text:
He will fulfill the desire of those who fear him.
He also will hear their cry, and will save them.
verses.explainer: A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

verses.text:
Yahweh preserves all those who love him,
but all the wicked he will destroy.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
My mouth will speak the praise of Yahweh.
Let all flesh bless his holy name forever and ever.
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

explainer.summary: A hymn of praise celebrating God's character, mighty deeds, and sovereign rule over all creation.
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 146 is a hymn -- a song of praise that exalts God for who He is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Praise Yah!
Praise Yahweh, my soul.
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
While I live, I will praise Yahweh.
I will sing praises to my God as long as I exist.
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
Don’t put your trust in princes,
each a son of man in whom there is no help.
verses.explainer: An expression of trust in God as refuge and protection. The Psalms repeatedly use fortress imagery -- God is a stronghold, a rock, a shield -- because the ancient world understood safety in terms of physical fortification.

verses.text:
His spirit departs, and he returns to the earth.
In that very day, his thoughts perish.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Happy is he who has the God of Jacob for his help,
whose hope is in Yahweh, his God:
verses.explainer: A plea for divine help that characterizes the lament genre. The psalmist does not suffer in silence but brings pain directly to God, trusting that God hears and acts on behalf of those who call.

A reference to God's heavenly throne and sovereign rule. The Psalms affirm that God governs from heaven while actively engaging with events on earth -- He is both transcendent and intimately involved.
A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Yahweh opens the eyes of the blind.
Yahweh raises up those who are bowed down.
Yahweh loves the righteous.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Yahweh preserves the foreigners.
He upholds the fatherless and widow,
but the way of the wicked he turns upside down.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

explainer.summary: A hymn of praise celebrating God's character, mighty deeds, and sovereign rule over all creation.
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 147 is a hymn -- a song of praise that exalts God for who He is and what He has done.

verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
Yahweh builds up Jerusalem.
He gathers together the outcasts of Israel.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
He heals the broken in heart,
and binds up their wounds.
verses.explainer: A verse addressing the heart -- the center of thought, will, and emotion in Hebrew understanding. The Psalms consistently teach that authentic worship begins in the inner life, not in external ritual.

verses.text:
He counts the number of the stars.
He calls them all by their names.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Great is our Lord, and mighty in power.
His understanding is infinite.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Yahweh upholds the humble.
He brings the wicked down to the ground.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
Sing to Yahweh with thanksgiving.
Sing praises on the harp to our God,
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
who covers the sky with clouds,
who prepares rain for the earth,
who makes grass grow on the mountains.
verses.explainer: Rock and mountain imagery representing stability, permanence, and strength. God is the 'Rock' -- the unshakable foundation when everything else is uncertain.

verses.text:
He provides food for the livestock,
and for the young ravens when they call.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
He doesn’t delight in the strength of the horse.
He takes no pleasure in the legs of a man.
verses.explainer: An expression of joy that reflects the Psalter's conviction that genuine happiness is found in God's presence, not in circumstances. Joy in the Psalms is often a choice made in the midst of difficulty.

verses.text:
Yahweh takes pleasure in those who fear him,
in those who hope in his loving kindness.
verses.explainer: An appeal to or celebration of God's steadfast love (chesed) -- His covenant faithfulness that endures regardless of human failure. Chesed is the most important word in the Psalms for describing God's character.

verses.text:
Praise Yahweh, Jerusalem!
Praise your God, Zion!
verses.explainer: A call to praise that reflects the Psalter's primary purpose: directing God's people toward worship. Praise in the Psalms is not just emotion but a deliberate choice to honor God regardless of circumstances.

verses.text:
For he has strengthened the bars of your gates.
He has blessed your children within you.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
He makes peace in your borders.
He fills you with the finest of the wheat.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
He sends out his commandment to the earth.
His word runs very swiftly.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
He gives snow like wool,
and scatters frost like ashes.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
He hurls down his hail like pebbles.
Who can stand before his cold?
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
He sends out his word, and melts them.
He causes his wind to blow, and the waters flow.
verses.explainer: Water imagery in the Psalms carries dual meaning: life-giving provision (streams, rain, still waters) and chaotic threat (floods, depths, raging seas). The psalmist trusts God over both.

verses.text:
He shows his word to Jacob;
his statutes and his ordinances to Israel.
verses.explainer: A verse of praise exalting God's character and deeds. Hymns in the Psalter call God's people to worship based on who God is and what He has done.

verses.text:
He has not done this for just any nation.
They don’t know his ordinances.
Praise Yah!

mountains and all hills;

explainer.summary: Let everything that has breath praise the LORD! Praise Him with trumpet, harp, lyre, tambourine, dancing, strings, pipe, and cymbals. The Psalter ends as it should: with all creation, all instruments, and all breath united in praise.
explainer.contextInBook: Psalm 150 is the grand finale of the entire Psalter. After 149 psalms of lament, praise, questioning, and thanksgiving, everything resolves into one word: praise. Every breath, every instrument, every creature -- all join the chorus.
